content:
# Revelation Chapter 10 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE, pages 284-285 | **Source Image:** REV_010_page_001.jpg, REV_010_page_002.jpg

1. And I saw another strong angel coming down out of the heaven, having been clothed with a cloud, and the rainbow [was] upon his head, and his face [was] as the sun, and his feet as pillars of fire,

2. and having in his hand a little scroll having been opened. And he placed his right foot upon the sea and the left upon the land,

3. and he cried out with a great voice as a lion roars. And when he cried out, the seven thunders spoke [with] their own voices.

4. And when the seven thunders spoke, I was about to write; and I heard a voice out of the heaven saying, "Seal the things which the seven thunders spoke, and do not write them."

5. And the angel whom I saw standing upon the sea and upon the land raised his right hand to the heaven,

6. and swore by the [One] living unto the ages of the ages, who created the heaven and the things in it, and the earth and the things in it, and the sea and the things in it, that time shall be no longer,

7. but in the days of the voice of the seventh angel, when he is about to sound [his trumpet], then the mystery of God is finished, as he proclaimed [the good news] to his own servants the prophets.

8. And the voice which I heard out of the heaven, again speaking with me and saying, "Go, take the scroll which has been opened in the hand of the angel who stands upon the sea and upon the land."

9. And I went to the angel, saying to him to give me the little scroll. And he says to me, "Take and eat it up, and it shall make your belly bitter, but in your mouth it shall be sweet as honey."

10. And I took the little scroll out of the hand of the angel and ate it up, and it was in my mouth sweet as honey; and when I ate it, my belly was made bitter.

11. And they say to me, "You must prophesy again concerning peoples and nations and tongues and many kings."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "having been clothed" (peribeblēmenon):** Perfect passive participle — the cloud wraps the angel as a garment. The imagery recalls Ezekiel 1:28 and the theophanic cloud.

2. **v.2 — "little scroll" (biblaridion):** Sinaiticus consistently uses the diminutive biblaridion throughout this chapter, distinguishing it from the larger biblion of chapter 5. This is a different, smaller document.

3. **v.3 — "as a lion roars" (hōsper leōn mukatai):** Sinaiticus reads mukatai — literally "bellows" or "roars." The comparison is direct and vivid.

4. **v.4 — "Seal...do not write":** The imperative sphragison (seal up) followed by mē grapsēs (do not write — aorist subjunctive with negative). John is commanded to conceal, in contrast to 22:10 where he is told not to seal.

5. **v.6 — "time shall be no longer" (chronos ouketi estai):** Sinaiticus reads chronos — time in the sense of delay or duration. This is not the end of time itself but the end of the delay before God's purposes are completed.

6. **v.7 — "proclaimed the good news" (euēngelisen):** The aorist of euangelizō — God "gospeled" his servants. The mystery has been the content of prophetic proclamation throughout history.

7. **v.9 — "he says to me" (legei moi):** Historic present tense in Sinaiticus — legei rather than eipen. This shift to present tense brings the reader directly into the visionary moment.

8. **v.11 — "they say to me" (legousin moi):** The plural subject is unidentified — a divine passive or heavenly chorus. Sinaiticus reads legousin clearly. The commission to prophesy "again" (palin) implies a renewed mandate.

9. **v.11 — "concerning" (epi):** Sinaiticus uses epi with dative — "upon/concerning" peoples and nations. The preposition carries the sense of prophetic authority directed toward these groups.

— EMET
